preamble: The shift from the voiceless velar fricative [x] to the voiceless uvular fricative [χ] (the voiceless counterpart of [ʁ]) is a form of backing or retraction, common in languages where back fricatives are realized with a more posterior constriction.

examples.note: Georgian ხ is standardly [x] (voiceless velar fricative), but in some eastern dialects and in connected speech before back vowels it is realized as uvular [χ]. Georgian also has a separate uvular stop series (ყ [q']).
Widespread shift where the historical velar 'hard G' is realized as a uvular fricative in standard and northern dialects.
Allophonic and dialectal variation where the velar fricative (from historical Chi) is realized as uvular before back vowels.
